did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a supervised machine learning approach based on Sparse Representation (SR) in the quaternion wavelet (QW) domain, specifically the SRCQW method, to classify skin lesions (melanoma vs. benign) using the ISIC2017 and ISIC2019 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL PAPER' and presents a novel supervised machine learning method (SRCQW) for skin lesion classification. It includes original experimental validation on the ISIC2017 and ISIC2019 datasets, detailed methodology, and comparative results, which are characteristic of original research rather than a review.

title: Skin lesion image classification using sparse representation in quaternion wavelet domain
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of automated melanoma classification by proposing a novel supervised machine learning method called SRCQW, which utilizes Sparse Representation in the Quaternion Wavelet domain. The method constructs dictionaries using quaternion coefficients from low, high, and mixed wavelet frequencies to classify skin lesions as melanoma or benign without requiring lesion segmentation. The authors validated the approach on the public ISIC2017 and ISIC2019 datasets, comparing its performance against contemporary deep learning neural networks. Experimental results on the ISIC2017 test set demonstrated that the SRCQW method achieved an accuracy of 95.2% and a Youden’s index of 79.8%, outperforming several state-of-the-art deep learning classifiers. The study concludes that SRCQW is particularly effective and balanced for diagnosing both benign and malignant lesions, especially when using middle-sized training sets.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as the positive class in the binary classification task. In ISIC2017, included 374 malignant images in training and 117 in test. In ISIC2019, malignant images were sampled from HAM10000/ISIC Archive to balance the dataset.
Classified as the negative class in the binary classification task. In ISIC2017, included 1626 benign images in training and 483 in test. The study focuses on distinguishing these from melanoma.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic categories: melanoma (malignant) vs. benign skin lesions
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'skin lesion image classification' and 'automated melanoma classification'. The model predicts discrete classes (melanoma or benign) using the SRCQW method on the ISIC2017 and ISIC2019 datasets. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as Accuracy, Specificity, Sensitivity, Precision, F1-score, and Youden’s J statistic.

datasets_used.name: ISIC2017
datasets_used.description: Public skin lesion image dataset from the ISBI 2017 challenge, equipped with gold standard diagnosis. The study used a subset of 2000 training images (balanced to 1000 benign and 1000 malignant by sampling from HAM10000/ISIC Archive) and 600 test images. It also had 150 validation images held out by the organizer.

use.training: Used to train the SRCQW dictionary and model. Specifically, 1000 benign and 1000 malignant images were randomly selected for experimental training.
use.validation: 150 images were held out by the ISBI 2017 organizer for validation purposes.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the final performance of the SRCQW method against contemporary methods and top challenge results.
use.other: Used to compare performance with top 10 deep NN-based classifiers from the ISIC2017 challenge.

datasets_used.name: ISIC2019
datasets_used.description: Public skin lesion dataset containing 25,331 dermoscopic images of eight different skin lesion classes, originally taken from HAM10000, BCN20000, and MSK datasets. The study focused on binary classification (melanoma vs. benign) using a subset of images.

use.training: Used for training via Monte Carlo cross-validation (c=10 splits). Specific subset sizes mentioned for comparison with ResNets were 10,000, 5,000, and 1,000 images.
use.validation: Used for validation within the Monte Carlo cross-validation framework (10% of selected training sets).
use.testing: Used for testing within the Monte Carlo cross-validation framework.
use.other: Used to validate the advantages of SRCQW over NNs with middle-sized training sets and to compare with ResNet architectures.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of dermoscopic images of pigmented skin lesions. Referenced as a source for balancing the ISIC2017 training set and as an origin for ISIC2019 images.

use.training: 626 malignant images were randomly selected from HAM10000/ISIC Archive to balance the ISIC2017 training set (to reach 1000 malignant images).

datasets_used.name: BCN20000
datasets_used.description: A dataset of dermoscopic lesions in the wild. Referenced as an origin for ISIC2019 images.

datasets_used.name: MSK dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset referenced as an origin for ISIC2019 images.

datasets_used.name: ISIC2020
datasets_used.description: Recent skin lesion image database containing 33,126 training and 10,982 test images. Used to demonstrate computational complexity and performance with a small training subset.

use.training: A small subset of 876 training images was used to test the method's efficiency with limited data.
use.testing: The full set of 10,982 test images was classified using the model trained on the 876 images.
use.other: Used to compare against methods that used the entire training set.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To validate the SRCQW method on the ISIC2019 dataset by randomly selecting training and test sets, averaging results over 10 splits to reduce variance of the split sample error estimate.
To formulate sparse coding in the quaternion wavelet domain, minimizing the residual between the test image and its sparse representation while preventing overfitting via an L1 regularization term.
To reduce the dimensionality of the dictionary quaternion vectors by retaining features based on cumulative energy.
To evaluate the balance of the classifier's performance for both benign and malignant lesions (calculated as Specificity + Sensitivity - 1).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics (Accuracy, Specificity, Sensitivity, etc.) and qualitative statements of superiority (e.g., 'outperforms', 'highest results') without statistical significance testing.

ISIC2017.experiment: SRCQW with different quaternion wavelet sub-bands
ISIC2017.notes: SRCQW with low-frequency sub-band L_L achieved the highest accuracy, specificity, and precision among contemporary methods. It ranked first in accuracy, sensitivity, miss rate, and Youden's index among top challenge participants. Mixed frequencies (L_L & H_L, L_L & H_H) provided the highest sensitivity for melanoma recognition.

ISIC2019.experiment: SRCQW vs ResNets with varying dataset sizes
ISIC2019.notes: SRCQW with a small training set (1,000 images) outperformed ResNet architectures trained with larger sets (10,000 images) in terms of Balanced Accuracy. SRCQW is noted to be efficient for middle-sized training sets.

ISIC2020.notes: The method used only 876 training images to classify 10,982 test images. Methods achieving higher results on ISIC2020 used the entire training set of 33,126 images.

architecture_used.main_model: SRCQW (Sparse Representation Classification in Quaternion Wavelet domain)
architecture_used.pretraining: None; the method is a supervised machine learning approach that constructs dictionaries from the training data using Quaternion Wavelet Transform (QWT) coefficients.
architecture_used.input_size: 256 x 192 pixels (cropped from original images); also tested with 128x128, 192x192, and 256x256 inputs on ISIC2019.

architecture_details.core_method: Sparse Representation Classification (SRC) in the Quaternion Wavelet (QW) domain.
architecture_details.transform: Quaternion Wavelet Transform (QWT) using the dual-tree quaternion wavelet to provide shift-invariance and richer geometric information than DWT.
architecture_details.dictionary_construction: Dictionaries are constructed using quaternion coefficients from low-frequency (L_L), high-frequency (L_H, H_L, H_H), and mixed sub-bands. The low-frequency sub-band L_L yielded the best performance.
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Feature dimension M1 = 3600 for single sub-bands; M2 = 4000 for mixed sub-bands.
architecture_details.optimization_algorithm: QFISTA (Quaternion Fast Iterative Shrinkage-Thresholding Algorithm), an extension of FISTA to minimize the QWLASSO model.
architecture_details.regularization: L1,2 norm regularization (QWLASSO model) to prevent overfitting.

hyperparameters.cross_validation: Monte Carlo cross-validation with c=10 splits for ISIC2019.

training_details.training_method: Dictionary learning via sparse coding on training images. No iterative neural network training; parameters are derived from the training set statistics for dictionary atoms.

dataset_splits.ISIC2017: 1000 benign and 1000 malignant images (balanced) for training; 600 images for testing.
dataset_splits.ISIC2019: Variable sizes (10,000, 5,000, 1,000 images) for training via Monte Carlo cross-validation; 10% of selected training sets used for validation.

classification_or_output_head.mechanism: Assigns the test sample to the class that provides the minimal residual between the test quaternion vector and its sparse representation in the dictionary.

architecture_used.approximate_parameters: Not explicitly reported as a single count; the method relies on dictionary atoms derived from the training set size (e.g., 2000 atoms for ISIC2017) and feature dimensions (3600 or 4000).

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: SRCQW (Sparse Representation Classification in Quaternion Wavelet domain)
SVM (combined with deep learning and sparse coding)
preprocessing_techniques: Cropping: 80% of the background was cropped from the center of each image to balance the images and focus on the lesion.
Filtering: A Laplacian filter (∇²I + 2I) was applied to the original images in the ISIC2019 dataset during the pre-processing stage.
conclusions: The proposed SRCQW method, which uses Sparse Representation in the Quaternion Wavelet domain, effectively classifies skin lesions as melanoma or benign without requiring lesion segmentation.
Constructing dictionaries using quaternions from the low-frequency wavelet sub-band (L_L) yields the most accurate classification performance compared to high-frequency or mixed sub-bands.

On the ISIC2017 test set, SRCQW achieved an accuracy of 95.2%, specificity of 98.6%, and Youden’s index of 79.8%, outperforming contemporary methods and top challenge participants in accuracy, specificity, and precision.

SRCQW demonstrates superior efficiency with middle-sized training sets; on the ISIC2019 dataset, it outperformed ResNet architectures (ResNet-18, ResNet-50, ResNet-152) trained with larger datasets (up to 10,000 images) when using a smaller training set (1,000 images).

The method is particularly balanced for diagnosing both benign and malignant lesions, as evidenced by its high Youden’s J statistic, making it suitable for clinical applications where class balance is critical.

While SRCQW with the low-frequency sub-band provided the highest overall accuracy and specificity, mixed frequencies (L_L & H_L, L_L & H_H) provided the highest sensitivity for melanoma recognition on the ISIC2017 dataset.
The use of the Quaternion Wavelet Transform (QWT) overcomes the lack of shift-invariance in standard Discrete Wavelet Transforms and provides richer geometric information for skin lesion analysis.
important_remarks: The study addresses class imbalance in the ISIC2017 dataset by artificially balancing the training set (1000 benign vs. 1000 malignant) using samples from HAM10000, which may not reflect real-world prevalence.
The method does not perform lesion segmentation, relying instead on cropping 80% of the background; this approach may be sensitive to image framing and background artifacts.

Performance comparisons with contemporary methods and top challenge participants on ISIC2017 are based on reported metrics from other papers rather than re-running their code, limiting the directness of the comparison.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for performance differences between SRCQW and competing methods.
SRCQW demonstrates high computational efficiency with small-to-medium training sets (e.g., 1,000 images) but acknowledges that using large training datasets requires significant computer resources.
The method was implemented in MATLAB, which may limit its deployment in standard deep learning frameworks or clinical software ecosystems that typically rely on Python-based libraries.

While SRCQW outperformed ResNets with smaller training sets (1,000 images) on ISIC2019, ResNets trained with larger sets (10,000 images) generally achieved higher balanced accuracy, indicating SRCQW's advantage is specific to data-constrained scenarios.

The study focuses on binary classification (melanoma vs. benign) and does not evaluate performance on other skin lesion classes present in the ISIC2019 dataset (e.g., seborrheic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma).
